derived_from: FIPS 180-4 — Secure Hash Standard (SHS), NIST, August 2015
RFC 1321 — The MD5 Message-Digest Algorithm, April 1992
Test vectors verified by running Python 3.14.3 hashlib and cross-checking with openssl dgst and sha256sum on macOS/Darwin 25.3.0, 2026-03-30
notes: SHA-256('abc') verified value: ba7816bf8f01cfea414140de5dae2223b00361a396177a9cb410ff61f20015ad. This was verified against Python 3.14.3, openssl dgst -sha256, and sha256sum; all three tools agreed. Some published references cite a different value; this spec uses only the machine-verified value.
SHA-256('') = e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855 — this matches the FIPS 180-4 reference.
MD5 and SHA-1 are algorithmically broken for cryptographic use but are behaviorally specified here for completeness and legacy compatibility.

library.notes: hashlib is a Python stdlib module. It delegates to OpenSSL if available, otherwise uses built-in implementations. The behavioral spec is the same regardless of which backend hashlib uses internally.

block_sizes_bytes.name: SHA256_BLOCK_SIZE
block_sizes_bytes.description: SHA-256 internal block size: 512 bits = 64 bytes

block_sizes_bytes.name: SHA1_BLOCK_SIZE
block_sizes_bytes.description: SHA-1 internal block size: 512 bits = 64 bytes

block_sizes_bytes.name: MD5_BLOCK_SIZE
block_sizes_bytes.description: MD5 internal block size: 512 bits = 64 bytes

block_sizes_bytes.name: SHA512_BLOCK_SIZE
block_sizes_bytes.description: SHA-512 internal block size: 1024 bits = 128 bytes

hash_object.description: Object returned by hashlib.new() and algorithm-specific constructors. All methods are safe to call on a finalised object; digest() and hexdigest() do not consume or reset the state.

fields.name: name
fields.description: Canonical algorithm name as recognised by hashlib.new() (read-only)

hashlib.new.description: Create a new hash object for the named algorithm. Equivalent to calling the algorithm-specific constructor (e.g. hashlib.sha256()).

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: Optional initial data to hash; equivalent to calling update(data) immediately after construction

parameters.name: usedforsecurity
parameters.description: If False, allows use of algorithms that might be blocked in FIPS mode. Default True.

return_values.hash_object: Initialised hash object ready for update() and digest() calls
return_values.ValueError: If name is not a recognised algorithm

hash.update.description: Feed data into the hash. May be called multiple times. Equivalent to calling new(name, concat(all_data_so_far)).
postconditions: hash.update(a); hash.update(b) produces the same final state as hash.update(a+b)

hash.digest.description: Return the hash of all data fed so far as a bytes object of length digest_size. Does not reset or consume the hash state; safe to call multiple times.

hash.hexdigest.description: Return the hash as a lowercase hex string of length digest_size * 2. Equivalent to h.digest().hex().

hash.copy.description: Return a copy of the hash object with the same internal state. Updates to the copy do not affect the original.

invariants.description: SHA-256 of empty input — verified against system Python/OpenSSL and sha256sum
SHA-256 of b'abc' — verified against system Python 3.14.3, openssl, and sha256sum
SHA-256 of 56-byte FIPS 180-4 Appendix B test message — verified against system tools
SHA-1 of empty input — FIPS 180-4
SHA-1 of b'abc' — FIPS 180-4
MD5 of empty input — RFC 1321 Appendix A.5 test 1
MD5 of b'abc' — RFC 1321 Appendix A.5 test 2
MD5 of the quick-brown-fox test string — widely cited reference vector
SHA-512 of empty input — FIPS 180-4
SHA-512 of b'abc' — FIPS 180-4
SHA-256 update('Hel') then update('lo') must equal SHA-256('Hello') one-shot
SHA-1 incremental update composition
MD5 incremental update composition
SHA-256 hash object must have digest_size == 32 (256 bits)
SHA-256 internal block size must be 64 bytes (512 bits)
SHA-1 digest_size must be 20 bytes (160 bits)
MD5 digest_size must be 16 bytes (128 bits)
SHA-512 digest_size must be 64 bytes (512 bits)
SHA-256 hash object .name must be 'sha256'
algorithms_guaranteed must contain the five algorithms required by PEP 247 and universally present: sha1, sha224, sha256, sha384, sha512, md5
h.digest() must equal bytes.fromhex(h.hexdigest()) — both representations of the same hash value
h.copy() must produce an independent object; updating the copy must not change the original's digest
hashlib.new('sha256', data) must produce same digest as hashlib.sha256(data)

sha256_output.description: SHA-256 produces a fixed 32-byte (256-bit) message digest. No framing, no header, no checksum — pure hash output.

fields.name: H0..H7
fields.description: Eight 32-bit words H0 through H7, each in big-endian byte order, concatenated. These are the final values of the hash state after processing all message blocks.

sha1_output.description: SHA-1 produces a fixed 20-byte (160-bit) message digest.

fields.name: H0..H4
fields.description: Five 32-bit words H0 through H4 in big-endian byte order.

md5_output.description: MD5 produces a fixed 16-byte (128-bit) message digest. Unlike SHA-*, MD5 uses little-endian word order.

fields.name: A..D
fields.description: Four 32-bit words A, B, C, D in little-endian byte order.

error_model.return_code_semantics: hashlib uses Python exceptions rather than integer return codes. All hash operations raise exceptions on error rather than returning error values.
error_model.stream_error_field: N/A — Python exceptions carry the error message
error_model.error_stickiness: Hash objects do not have an error state. If update() raises (e.g. due to wrong type), the object's state is unchanged and the exception is propagated.

error_codes.name: ValueError
error_codes.meaning: Unknown algorithm name passed to hashlib.new(). Raised when the requested algorithm is not available.

error_codes.name: TypeError
error_codes.meaning: Non-bytes object passed to update(). hashlib requires bytes-like objects.
